segments.s768-g73: This is a prepositional/nominal phrase: 'بهر' (for/to) introduces the scope 'کشوری' (a country). 'در' is the locative preposition 'in', and 'ستمگاره‌ای' is an indefinite noun 'an oppressor' (the -ای ending marks indefiniteness). Word order is poetic (preposition before noun phrases); the whole segment means 'in/for a country, an oppressor'.
segments.s768-g74: 'پدید آید' is a two-word verbal idea meaning 'comes into view / appears' (پدید = appear, آید = comes); the verb is perfective/past in form but here reports an event. 'و' = 'and'. 'زشت' is an attributive adjective meaning 'vile/indecent' and qualifies the following noun. 'پتیاره‌ای' is an indefinite noun of uncertain/obsolete meaning; metrically it ends with -ای for indefiniteness. The clause therefore adds 'and a vile patiāreh appears'.
segments.s768-g75: 'نشان شب تیره' is a nominal phrase meaning 'a sign of (the) dark night' — Persian often omits the ezāfe in poetry. 'آمد پدید' is an inverted verbal sequence (آمد = came, پدید = forth/into view) meaning 'came forth/appeared'; inversion is stylistic and both verbs combine to indicate the event.
segments.s768-g76: همی is an emphatic/aspective particle here (classical usage giving an ongoing or imminent sense). 'روشنایی' is the noun 'light/brightness'. 'بخواهد' is an auxiliary-like verb meaning 'will/would try' or 'will attempt', and 'پرید' (literally 'to leap') is the main verb; together they yield 'is about to leap' or 'will try to spring forth'. The order is deliberate for meter: particle + noun + verbal cluster.
segments.s768-g77: کنون = 'now' sets the time. ما is the subject 'we'. به + دستوری functions as an instrumental/prepositional phrase 'by an order' (دستوری here = 'an order/command'). رهنمای is a nominal 'guide' used as the complement; note the verb that would explicitly mark 'made/made into' is implied here or linked to the following clause — classical Persian often allows such ellipsis for stylistic flow.
segments.s768-g78: همه is a universal quantifier 'all'. پهلوانان is the plural noun 'champions/heroes'. پاکیزه‑رای is a compound adjective 'pure‑minded / of pure opinion' modifying پهلوانان; adjective follows the noun in this line for metrical/style reasons. Together they form the object/complement 'all the pure‑minded champions'.
segments.s768-g79: به سوی خراسان is a directional prepositional phrase 'toward Khorasan' (به سوی = toward). نهادیم is the past 1st‑person plural verb 'we set/placed' (here idiomatically 'we set/turned (our) face'). روی is the noun 'face' used here in the idiom 'نهادنِ روی' = 'to turn one's face' (i.e., to go/turn toward). The verb is explicit here and links back to the previous clause's role 'we made ... guides'.
segments.s768-g80: بر introduces the target of the turning ('toward' / 'against' in context). مرزبانان is the plural 'border‑governors / frontier guards'. دیهیم‑جوی is a compound describing them as 'seekers of diyah (blood‑money)', i.e., avengers/demanders of compensation; it modifies مرزبانان. The whole phrase names the group toward whom they set their face.
segments.s768-g161: دگر here is an adverb meaning 'once again' (an iterative/adversative particle) placed before the noun. پیکرش is پیکر 'body/figure' with the third‑person possessive suffix -ش 'his', so 'his body'. افسر (here 'helmet/diadem') and چهر 'face/visage' are coordinated by و 'and' with no copula explicit; the copular meaning ('is / becomes') is implied across the noun phrase.
segments.s768-g162: زمین 'the earth/land' is the noun subject; بارور 'fertile' is an adjective/predicative adjective. گشته is the perfect/passive participial/coprular form of شدن 'to become' (here 'has become'), so زمین بارور گشته = 'the earth has become fertile'. از 'from' marks cause/agent and مهر ما 'our love' is the source/cause ('from our love').
segments.s768-g163: به here functions as a preposition meaning 'for/on the occasion of' (به نوروز و مهر = 'for Nowruz and Mehr'). نوروز and مهر are coordinated by و. آن هم 'that/it too' emphasizes that the same thing is adorned; آراستست is the archaic/poetic copula form آراسته‌ست 'is adorned' (آراست + است with the old contraction).
segments.s768-g164: دو جشن 'two festivals' is a noun phrase with numeral + noun. بزرگست is the copula + adjective construction (بزرگ + است) meaning 'is great'. The conjunction و links the next phrase. با here ('with/by') combines with خواستست (خواست + است) — an archaic construction meaning 'is by desire' or 'is (made) with will/consent'; syntactically خواست is a noun 'will/desire' and -ست is the copula.
segments.s768-g165: درود جهان is a genitive phrase 'the world's blessing/greeting' (درود governed by جهان). بر is the preposition 'upon/on' and کم‌آزار‌مرد is a compound adjective+noun meaning 'a man who does little harm' or 'harmless man' (literally 'little‑harmer man'); the whole phrase is a benedictory formula 'blessing of the world upon the harmless man'.
segments.s768-g166: کسی 'someone' begins a relative clause; کو is an archaic relative particle here functioning like 'who/that' (not the locative 'where') — grammatically it introduces the clause 'who...'. ز is the poetic form of از 'from', marking source or relation. دیهیم modifies ما ('we, our') and means 'rural/villagers' (so ز دیهیمِ ما = 'from our countryside/our village people'). یاد 'remembrance' is a noun and کرد is the light verb 'did/made' used with یاد to mean 'remembered' or 'made mention of'.
segments.s768-g167: This is a stacked adjectival + attributive phrase modifying سوار (rider). بلَند is an adjective 'tall/high' placed before the following modifiers; اختری ('star‑like') is another adjectival epithet; نامجویی is a deverbal/agentive noun used attributively meaning 'one who seeks a name/renown'. In Persian poetry adjectives and attributive nouns commonly appear directly before the head noun without the ezāfe connector, so all three words together describe the rider.
segments.s768-g168: بیامد is the simple past 'came'. به introduces a directional/locative phrase here ('into/toward'), and کف literally 'palm/hand' is used figuratively with نامه ('record/book') to mean 'into the hands/record of'. نامه شهریار is a genitive construction 'the king's book/record' (poetic use of نامه for register). Together the phrase indicates that the rider (or his name/story) entered the royal register or came into the king's hands.
segments.s768-g121: همان is a demonstrative determiner ('that' / 'the same') placed before a list of nouns. The three nouns ارزن، پسته، ناردان are joined by و ('and') in the normal Persian coordinating order; ناردان here is a commodity (spikenard), not a proper name.
segments.s768-g122: بیارند is an older/classical third‑person verb meaning 'bring' (here 'brought' or 'brought/bring'); the exact lemma in the supplied list did not include this sense, so it is marked -1. یکی before a noun often means 'one' or 'a certain' ('one skilled mobad'). موبدی is the title 'mobad' (Zoroastrian priest) and کاردان is an adjective 'skilled/able' following the noun to qualify it.
segments.s768-g123: شتروار is an adverbial/qualifying word meaning 'in a long file / camel-train' and precedes the following nouns. زین here refers to the saddle or saddled pack animals, هر یکی = 'each one' or 'apiece', and ده‌هزار is the numeral 'ten thousand' attached to 'each'.
segments.s768-g124: هیونان is the plural 'beasts / animals' and is the subject of the following verb. بختی here is adjectival/adverbial ('fortunate' / possibly intensifying), modifying the clause. بیارند is the same classical verb 'bring' (here 'they brought' or 'they bear'); the supplied senses did not include this verbal meaning and so is marked -1. بار is the noun 'load / cargo' that the beasts carry.
segments.s768-g125: همان again is the demonstrative 'that/the same'. گاو گردون is a poetic compound ('gaav' + 'gardoon') — 'heavenly/sky cow' — where گردون qualifies گاو; هزار is the numeral 'a thousand' that counts the compound, and از نمک ('from/of salt') indicates the material or source.
segments.s768-g126: بیارند is the classical verb 'bring' (here 'they brought') and is again not matched in the provided senses, so marked -1. تا introduces the result/purpose clause ('so that' / 'until') and here connects to the following verb phrase. بر combines with گردد to form the sense 'turn over / be turned against/over', چه is an interrogative/exclamatory element here functioning like 'how' or 'that', and گردد is the subjunctive/present classical form of 'to turn / become' with فلک ('the sky/heaven') as the affected noun; together the clause means roughly 'so that the heavens would turn.'
segments.s768-g127: ز (poetic form of 'از') means 'from' and introduces the source/commodity: 'from dates a thousand and from sugar a thousand'. The conjunction و links the two parallel prepositional phrases; each هزار is the numeral 'a thousand' following the noun it counts.
segments.s768-g128: بود is the past/copular 'was' (here stating the situation). سخته is an adjective/adverb 'hard/difficult' describing the counting; و links the two adjectives/phrases. راست‌کرده is a compound participle 'set straight / made right / arranged properly' modifying شمار ('count/tally'), which is the noun governed by بود — 'the tally was difficult and made straight/accurate.'
segments.s768-g153: بدین روزگار = 'in/at these times'; the prepositional prefix ب(به) + demonstrative (here mis‑tokenized) yields a temporal expression. روزگار is the noun 'time/age' and the two adjectives تباه و دژم ('ruined' and 'sullen/gloomy') follow it in typical Persian order (noun then modifiers), describing the state of the age.
segments.s768-g154: بیابد is a third‑person present (old poetic form) meaning 'he finds/obtains' (here indicative/subjunctive flavour in narration). ز introduces the source 'from', گنجور is the treasury/treasure‑store (object of ز), ما is the possessor 'our', and چل درم is the quantity 'forty dirhams' (چل = forty, درم = dirham coin). Word order is source (ز گنجور ما) then amount (چل درم).
segments.s768-g155: به سنگ کسی کو بود زیردست is an inverted, archaic clause introducing the person: کسی 'someone' with the relative/archaic کو (here functioning as a relative 'who') and بود 'was' place the predicate 'زیردست' ('an underling/subordinate') after it. به سنگ is a short locative/illustrative phrase ('by/at a stone' or similar idiom) placed before the relative clause for poetic rhythm. Note the archaic word order and the relative کو.
segments.s768-g156: یکی = 'one' / 'a certain (one)'. زین + درمها... here in the poem is the conditional idea 'اگر آید بدست' — 'if one of these dirhams comes into (someone's) hand'. آید is a present/optative 'comes' or 'should come', and بدست ('in the hand') completes the verb 'come into the hand'. The compound درمها‌گر appears in the manuscript as joined pieces; syntactically read as درم‌ها + گر ('if dirhams...').
segments.s768-g157: از آن شست = 'from that thumb' (از = 'from/of', آن = 'that', شست = 'thumb'). بر سر here is a locative/measure phrase 'on/at the head' (بر = 'on', سر = 'head'); شش و چاردانگ is a compound numeral/measure 'six and a quarter' (شش = six, چاردانگ = 'a fourth/share' = a quarter). The sequence gives a measured quantity associated with the body‑mark or fee described.
segments.s768-g158: بیارد (here manuscript form) is a verbal idea 'he brings/takes' (the lemma supplied in the wordlist seems mismatched), نبشته = 'written text/inscription', and بخواند is a present/imperative‑like form 'he reads/recites'. به بانگ = 'with a cry / aloud' (به functioning adverbially + بانگ 'cry/voice'). The clause means he takes the inscribed tablet and proclaims or reads it aloud.
segments.s768-g159: بیک روی is a poetic phrase meaning 'on one side/one face' (بِـ + یک روی = 'on one side'); the word order places the locative phrase before the noun. بر نام یزدان پاک = 'upon the name of the Pure God' (بر = 'on/upon', نام = 'name', یزدان پاک = 'Pure God'). Note the devotional formula: name + divine epithet.
segments.s768-g160: کزویست is a fused relative/particle meaning 'from whom/whence' (introducing the divine source). امید = 'hope'; و ... ترس و باک lists parallel emotional effects: 'and from him fear and dread' (ز = 'from', ترس = 'fear', باک = 'dread/concern'). The line attributes both consolation and awe to the deity in balanced, parallel structure.
segments.s768-g137: به is a preposition marking direction/goal and combines with the noun دیدار to mean 'to the meeting/for the visit.' The phrase places the preposition before the noun phrase (preposition + noun), with پیران و فرهنگیان as a coordinated noun phrase 'elders and cultured/learned men.'
segments.s768-g138: بزرگان is an appositive or resumed subject 'the great ones.' که‌اند is the relative particle که plus the 3rd-person plural copula اند ('are'), forming 'who are.' از introduces origin ('from'), and کنارنگیان names the group/place they belong to. The relative clause modifies بزرگان: 'great ones who are from Konarng.'
segments.s768-g139: به دو روز functions as a temporal phrase 'on the second day' (classical usage places the numeral after the preposition), followed by نامه (letter/document) and then the destination phrase به دژها 'to the fortresses.' نهند is a classical 3rd-person plural verb meaning 'they put/send/place' (here 'they send'). Word order places the temporal phrase and objects before the verb.
segments.s768-g140: یکی is an indefinite 'one / a certain' introducing 'one [item].' نامه گنجور is a noun (نامه) modified postpositively by the adjective گنجور 'treasure-laden / treasure-book.' ما را is the clitic pronoun 'us' with the object marker را placed before the verb, and دهند is 3rd-person plural 'they give.' The clause thus means 'they give us one treasure-filled book.'
segments.s768-g141: دگر here means 'the other/the rest.' خود and خویشتن are emphatic reflexives ('for themselves / to themselves'), joined by با 'with' to mark accompaniment; بدارند is the 3rd‑person plural present 'they keep / they hold.' The sequence places the object/emphatic pronouns before the verb, a common classical order.
segments.s768-g142: بزرگان begins a relative clause resumed by که 'who/that.' باشند is the 3rd-person plural copular form 'are / be' (classical subjunctive/indicative form used here as 'are'), and زان انجمن 'of that assembly' gives the membership. Together: 'the elders who are of that assembly.'
segments.s768-g143: همانا is an emphatic adverb 'verily/truly.' بران is a locative/particle meaning 'upon that' or 'on that place,' followed by two coordinated nouns راغ and کوه بلند ('Rāgh' [or 'longing' as a noun] and 'the high mountain'). The nouns are joined by و; بلند is a postpositive adjective modifying کوه.
segments.s768-g144: ز ... و ز ... is the repeated poetic preposition 'from/of' (ز) marking sources: 'from the Turk and from the Tazi (Arab).' نیاید is a negative present-form verb 'does not come / will not come,' and گزند is the noun 'harm.' The clause means 'no harm comes from the Turks or the Arabs.'
segments.s768-g129: This chunk gives the quantity and the goods: 'ده و دو هزار' (twelve thousand) is implied before the listed items. انگبین is a substantive meaning 'honey/nectar' and کندره is another noun 'frankincense/resin' placed after it; the connective و links them (و = 'and'). The number phrase precedes these nouns in Persian word order.
segments.s768-g130: بدژها is the plural agent 'the fortresses' (subject or source). کشند is a 3rd‑person plural verb form; here it functions as a verbal predicate meaning 'they bring / they draw' (an older/poetic use of کشیدن), so the forts are the source or carriers. آن همه یکسره is an adverbial phrase modifying the verb: آن = 'that', همه = 'all/altogether' and یکسره = 'completely/at once'.
segments.s768-g131: نمک خورده is a compound adjective: نمک 'salt' + خورده (past participle of خوردن) = 'salt‑eaten' = 'salted' (i.e. preserved). سر پوست here means 'pelts/heads of hide' (skin/hide as cargo); چون چل‌هزار is a comparison/quantity phrase 'like/as forty thousand' meaning 'as many as forty thousand' — Persian often places the adjective/participle before/after the noun in flexible order in poetry.
segments.s768-g132: بیارند is the verb 'they bring' (3rd‑person plural); it takes آن را as a marked object: آن = 'that/them' and را is the definite object marker. The relative/causal clause که آید به کار follows: که 'that/which' + آید (subjunctive/present 'comes') + به کار 'to/use' meaning 'so that it may be of use' or 'which will be useful'.
segments.s768-g133: شتروار سیصد is a quantified phrase: شتروار (literally 'in camel‑file' or 'by camel‑loads') + سیصد 'three hundred' = 'three hundred camel‑loads'. ز + زربفت شاه is a genitive construction 'of the king's gold‑brocade' (ز = poetic 'of/from'). Word order places the measure phrase before the source/possessor.
segments.s768-g134: بیارند is again the verb 'they bring/they load' (3rd‑person plural). بر بارها is a locative/goal phrase: بر = 'on/upon' and بارها = 'the loads/wagons' (bar meaning 'load'); تا دو ماه indicates duration 'for up to/two months' or 'over two months'. The whole segment says these brocades are carried on the loads over a two‑month period.
segments.s768-g135: بیاید is an optative/subjunctive 3rd‑person singular form meaning 'may come' or simply 'comes' in narrative; یکی موبدی = 'a certain mobed / one mobed' (یکی used as 'a certain'). با گروه = 'with a group/party'. The clause introduces a person arriving with companions.
segments.s768-g136: This is a locative source phrase: ز = 'from' (poetic), گاه شمیران names a place or district (here 'the Shemiran area') and و از رابه‌کوه adds another source 'and from Rab‑e‑Kuh' (رابه‌کوه treated as a toponym). The prepositions mark origin of the arriving group.
segments.s768-g81: ببینیم is 1st‑person plural subjunctive/imperfect used as a hortative ('let us see'). تا introduces the clause of purpose/result ('so that/let us see'). گردش روزگار is a genitive noun phrase (گردشِ روزگار) meaning 'the turn/course of fate' with the noun order typical of Persian poetry (head noun گردش followed by مؤخر 'روزگار').
segments.s768-g82: چه is the interrogative 'what' introducing a free reported question; گوید is 3rd‑person present (classical usage functioning like a future/’will say’). بدین = به + این ('to/with this') is tokenized here as ب + دین, where دین is not the noun 'religion' but part of the demonstrative construction; رای is the noun 'counsel/opinion', modified by the adjective نااستوار 'unstable', which follows it (typical adjective position).
segments.s768-g83: پس اکنون is a discourse sequence ('so/then, now'). ز بهر is an archaic prepositional phrase meaning 'for the sake of' (ز = poetic 'from/for', بهر = 'for'). کنارنگ here is an old title/place name (Kanarang) rather than the literal adjective 'beside'; طوس names the place Tūs and forms a genitive relation (کنارنگِ طوس). The whole phrase gives the purpose/beneficiary of the movement.
segments.s768-g84: بدین سو = به + این + سو ('toward this side/direction'); note the tokenization splits ب and دین but functionally this is بدین 'to/at this'. کشیدیم is past 1pl ('we drew/advanced/moved') and governs the following instruments/objects. پیلان و کوس are plural/compound instruments ('elephants and war‑drums') placed after the verb, a normal Persian word order here.
segments.s768-g85: فرخ‌زاد is the personal name (subject). با ما = 'with us'. ز یک پوست is an idiomatic genitive phrase ('of one skin' = of the same clan/kin); پوستست is the contracted poetic form of پوست است ('is of skin' → 'is of the same skin'), combining noun + copula into one token as often in classical verse.
segments.s768-g86: به پیوستگی functions adverbially ('in/for attachment/loyalty'); نیز هم is emphatic duplication meaning 'also/likewise'. دوستست is another contracted poetic form (دوست است) meaning 'is a friend' or 'is dear'. Together this gives 'he is also a friend in loyalty/close ties.'
segments.s768-g87: بالتونیه‌ست is a fused poetic predicate meaning 'he is in a balatoni/coat' (بالتونی + است). The predicate appears before the pronoun او (a permissible classical order), and کنون 'now' timestamps the action. رزمجوی is a substantive/adjectival noun 'battle‑seeking' or 'combatant,' describing his current state.
segments.s768-g88: سوی = 'toward'; جنگ دشمن is a genitive phrase 'the enemy's war/battle' (جنگِ دشمن). نهادست is a compound/past sense of نهاد + است ('has set/has placed' or 'is set'), here meaning 'has set' (i.e. has fixed). روی ('face') is the object of نهادست: نهادستِ روی = 'he set his face' (i.e. he turned his face toward).
segments.s768-g145: شما را functions as a benefactive/dative construction ('for you' / 'may it be for you') with را marking the person affected rather than a simple definite object. بدین is the prepositional prefix ب- + (این) meaning 'in this' and introduces the time phrase; روزگار is the noun 'time/age' followed by the adjective سترگ ('great/august') as a postpositive/attributive modifier. Word order is pronoun + (dative) + temporal prepositional phrase + head noun + adjective.
segments.s768-g146: یکی is an indefinite numeral 'one/a certain', modifying the noun دست ('hand' here figuratively meaning influence or power). باشد is the 3rd-person present subjunctive/jussive of بودن used optatively ('may there be'). بر ما is a prepositional phrase 'upon/over us' and بزرگ is an adjective ('great') functioning as a predicate or modifier of دست. Overall: 'May there be one great hand over us.'
segments.s768-g147: هنرمند is an adjective or noun meaning 'skillful'/'skilled person' that here qualifies گوینده ('speaker, orator'). دستور ما is a possessed noun phrase 'our command/instruction' with the possessor pronoun ما following the head noun, the normal classical Persian order.
segments.s768-g148: بفرماید is a polite/honorific 3rd-person jussive of فرمودن/فرما دادن used here as 'let him/her command/perform/say' (an optative/imperative sense). اکنون is the adverb 'now'. به + گنجورِ ما is a prepositional phrase 'to/into our treasury' with ما as the possessive following the noun. The verb fronting (بفرماید) plus the following time and prepositional phrase is typical classical order.
segments.s768-g149: که introduces a subordinate/conditional clause ('if' / 'whoever'). هر کس is 'anyone/whomever' as the subject; این را is the definite object 'this' marked by را. ندارد is the negative present 'does not have'. به رنج ('with hardship' or 'at some cost') is an adverbial phrase modifying the verb (how he should send).
segments.s768-g150: فرستد is a 3rd-person jussive/optative of فرستادن here meaning 'let him send' (or 'he should send'). ورا is the unstressed form 'to him'/'him' (object/beneficiary). پارسی جامه پنج is a noun phrase 'five Persian garments' in classical order: adjective (پارسی) + noun (جامه) with the numeral پنج placed after the noun (a common older order). The clause issues the instruction to send those garments to him.
segments.s768-g151: یکی 'one/a certain' introduces the item: خوب سربند 'a fine headband' (خوب before the noun as an attributive adjective). پیکر 'for the body/figure' specifies the intended use or recipient of the headband. به زر means 'of/with gold' (بـ used here to indicate material or ornamentation). The whole phrase names the reward/object to be received.
segments.s768-g152: بیابند is the 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive of یافتن used optatively/predictively ('they will find/obtain'). فرجام 'the end/outcome' is the direct object meaning 'as the result'. زین here functions as 'from/of this' (classical contraction 'زِ این' = 'from this'), linking to کار 'affair/deed' (زین کار = 'from this deed'); the final بر is a short particle meaning 'upon'/'as for', completing the poetic phrase 'بیابند فرجام زین کار بر' = 'they shall obtain as the outcome of this deed'. Note: the lemma زین in the provided list also has other senses (e.g. 'saddle') but here it is the prepositional/form 'زِ این'.
segments.s768-g105: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from/after' introducing the manner; 'هر گونه' is a noun phrase 'every kind/way' (هر modifies گونه). 'گفتیم و انداختیم' are two coordinated past verbs: گفتیم 'we spoke/discussed' and انداختیم here has an idiomatic, causative/resolutive sense 'we put/decided (it)', so together they mean 'we debated and settled (it)'.
segments.s768-g106: سرانجام is an adverb 'at last/finally'; یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/altogether'. بر + ین functions as the demonstrative/adverbial 'برین' = 'so/thus/into that' (ین is the orthographic variant of این). ساختیم is the simple past plural 'we made/constructed/decided on'. Together: 'in the end we entirely made it so.'
segments.s768-g107: که introduces a relative clause 'that/which'. The list uses repeated prepositions از/ز ('from') in poetic style: 'از تاج و ز تخت و مهر و نگین' = 'from crown and from throne and (from) seal and gem'. مهر here means 'seal/signet' rather than 'love'. There is no finite verb explicitly stated (a common poetic omission).
segments.s768-g108: همان is a demonstrative 'the same/that very' modifying جامه 'garment/robe'. روم و کشمیر و چین are coordinated place-names giving origin (Rome/Byzantium, Kashmir, China). The clause again lacks an explicit verb; the meaning is supplied from context ('that was/from the garments of ...').
segments.s768-g109: ز introduces the source/partitive 'of' (poetic). پرمایه (literally 'full of substance') functions as an adjective 'rich/valuable/substantial' modifying چیزی 'something/thing'. که آمد بدست is a relative clause 'that came into (our) hands' — آمد is past 'came' and بدست here means 'into the hand/into possession'.
segments.s768-g110: A further specification of source: 'ز روم و ز طایف' = 'from Rome and from the tribes/peoples'. همه هرچ هست is a summative clause 'all, whatever there is' (هرچ 'whatever', هست as the copula/existential 'is'). The sequence lists origins and then totals the goods.
segments.s768-g111: همان هرچه = 'that same whatever' (determiner + indefinite). از ما indicates separation 'from us' (i.e., away from our possession). پراگند نیست is a negative predication 'is not scattered/ dispersed' meaning 'remains with us/not lost'.
segments.s768-g112: گر is a conditional 'if'. از پوشش است = 'if it is of/for the covering' (پوشش 'covering; cloak/garment'). ار here is a second conditional/conditional particle used stylistically to introduce the conclusion; افگندنیست is an archaic negative modal formation meaning 'must not be thrown / is not to be cast away'. Overall: 'if it is part of a covering, then it must not be thrown away.'
segments.s768-g9: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from/of' introducing the domain ('from his covenant and command'). The noun phrase 'پیمان و فرمان او' is a coordinated genitive with the enclitic pronoun 'او' ('his') applying to both 'پیمان' and 'فرمان'. The verb 'نگذرد' is present/factual negative ('does not pass' / 'does not transgress') and refers back to that noun phrase.
segments.s768-g10: This clause reads as a single unit: 'دم خویش' = 'a moment/breath of himself' (possessive formed by the separate clitic 'ش' = 'his'); 'بی رای او' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'without his counsel/opinion' (بی = without + رای = counsel). The verb 'نشمرد' is a negative present ('does not reckon/consider'); together the clause means he will not regard even a moment as valid apart from his counsel.
segments.s768-g11: 'ز' here introduces origin/lineage ('of the king'); 'شاه جهان' is a compound title 'king of the world'. The proper name 'یزدگرد' follows and is qualified by the postpositive adjective 'بزرگ' ('the Great'), a common poetic ordering where titles and adjectives cluster around the name.
segments.s768-g12: This is a string of epithets: 'پدر' ('father') used honorifically, followed by the adjective 'نامور' ('renowned'), then 'شهریار' ('sovereign/king'). The final word 'سترگ' here functions as an additional epithet; its placement after the noun is typical of Persian epithets in poetic style.
segments.s768-g13: 'سپهدار' is a title meaning 'commander' and serves as the head noun; it is modified by the compound epithet 'یزدان پیروزگر' where 'یزدان' (God) qualifies 'پیروزگر' (triumphant/victorious), a poetic noun-noun adjective sequence forming 'God-victorious' or 'victorious by God'.
segments.s768-g14: 'نگهبان' ('protector/guardian') is followed by a coordinated series of objects: 'جنبده' (living creatures, i.e. animate beings), then 'بوم و بر' (a set phrase meaning homeland/land and domain). The conjunctions 'و' simply link the list; word order is noun (guardian) + its objects.
segments.s768-g15: 'ز تخم' = 'from the seed/lineage of' (a common poetic way to introduce descent). 'بزرگان یزدان‌شناس' is a noun phrase 'the great [bachelors/elders] who know God' (i.e., renowned pious ones); adjective-noun order here follows Persian conventions with the descriptive compound after the head.
segments.s768-g16: 'که' is the relative pronoun 'who/which'; 'از تاج دارند' literally 'have from the crown' — idiomatically 'who possess a crown'; the following prepositional phrase 'از اختر سپاس' indicates source of praise/blessing ('praise/admiration from the star[s]'). 'دارند' is present tense 'they have/possess', so the clause describes those descendants whose crown is honored by the stars.
segments.s768-g17: 'کزیشان' is the archaic compound of 'کز' (from/whence) + the enclitic pronoun 'شان' (them), so it means 'from them'. 'شد' is the past verb 'became' and pairs with the following adjective 'آباد' ('prosperous') to mean 'became prosperous/was made flourishing.' 'روی زمین' is a locative phrase: 'روی' (surface/face) + 'زمین' (earth), giving 'the face of the earth' or 'upon the earth.' Word order is typical classical Persian (verbal predicate 'شد' before adjectival complement and then locative), and the parts form one clause: 'from them (it) became prosperous on the earth.'
segments.s768-g18: 'فروزنده' is a participial/adjectival form meaning 'illuminating' or 'radiant' and here qualifies the following list of items; it functions as a predicate modifier. The nouns 'تاج و تخت و نگین' are simply enumerated (crown, throne, and gem) and are linked by the coordinating 'و' (and). In this poetic order the adjective precedes the list it describes, giving 'the crown, the throne, and the jewel, radiant.'
segments.s768-g19: 'سوی مرزبانان' is a prepositional/directional phrase: 'سوی' (toward) + 'مرزبانان' (frontier guardians/border‑governors, plural). 'با گنج' means 'with treasure' (instrumental 'با' + noun). 'گاه' here is used adverbially ('occasionally/at times'), so the phrase reads 'toward the frontier guardians with treasure and at times...'; the coordinate 'و' links 'گنج' and 'گاه' as two accompanying circumstances.
segments.s768-g20: The clause begins with the relative/connector 'که' ('who/which/that') introducing what they do: 'که با فر و برزند' = 'who/that with (their) farra/glory come/appear.' 'با' again marks accompaniment; 'فر' is the Zoroastrian/royal concept 'farr' (glory). 'برزند' is an archaic 3rd‑pl. verb form here meaning 'come/arrive' (classical usage), not modern 'tremble'; it describes the action of those addressed. The second coordinate 'و با داد و راه' lists further accompaniments: 'with justice and (the) way/right path.' The clause strings instruments/accompaniments with repeated 'با.'
segments.s768-g21: This is a nominal list of places: 'شمیران' (Shemirān) 'و' (and) 'رویین‌دژ' (iron/impregnable fortress) 'و' 'رابه‌کوه' (a fused form meaning 'to/into the mountain'—here a toponymic/directional phrase). The items are simply coordinated; 'رابه‌کوه' reflects an older fusion of the object/particle and preposition ('را به کوه' contracted) used poetically for place/direction.
segments.s768-g22: 'کلات' is a place/stronghold name. 'از دگر دست' is a small prepositional phrase: 'از' (from) + 'دگر دست' (other hand/side/quarter) meaning 'from another quarter.' The final phrase 'و دیگر گروه' is 'and another group/company' (group = troop here). Classical Persian often packs these spatial/agentive phrases together without additional particles; 'دست' with 'دگر' functions idiomatically as 'side/party.'
segments.s768-g23: 'نگهبان ما باد پروردگار' is an optative/invocatory clause in classical Persian. 'نگهبان ما' ('guardian/protector of us') is the subject phrase, 'باد' is the optative particle meaning 'may (it be),' and 'پروردگار' is the predicate 'the Lord.' Word order is inverted for poetic effect: literally 'Protector of us may (be) the Lord.'
segments.s768-g24: 'شما بی‌گزند' addresses the addressees ('you') followed by the adjective 'بی‌گزند' ('unharmed/safe'). 'از بد روزگار' is a prepositional phrase 'from the ill/bad fate of time' (i.e., 'from the evils of fate/time') with 'از' marking source/cause. The whole segment is a benediction: 'May you be unharmed from the evils of fate.'
segments.s768-g41: ز here is the poetic preposition meaning 'for' / 'because of' and introduces the reason or purpose. پاداش نیکی is a genitive phrase 'reward of goodness' (پاداش head + نیکی in the genitive). فزایش کنیم is a first-person-plural subjunctive/hortative ('let us increase' / 'we should increase'); the verb کنیم (here meaning 'do/make') follows the noun phrase, as is normal in Persian.
segments.s768-g42: برین is بر + ـین (here functioning like 'on/for this' or 'in this [matter]'); it frames the following noun as a specific instance ('in this initiative'). پیش‌دستی is a noun meaning 'preemption/initiative' and functions as the object of the hortative. نیایش کنیم repeats the hortative/imperative 'let us pray' (کنیم = 'let us do'), so the clause means 'let us, in this precedence, perform prayer.'
segments.s768-g43: همانا is an emphatic adverb 'verily/indeed.' که here introduces the clause (functioning like 'that' / a marker linking to the following information). آمد is the past tense 'came.' شما را uses را as the object/dative marker ('to you'); خبر is the noun 'news' and is the thing that 'came' — the order is poetic (object following the verb or placed before it for emphasis), but the meaning is 'news came to you.'
segments.s768-g44: که begins the embedded clause 'that...' ما را uses را to mark 'us' (the affected party). چه آمد is an interrogative‑style phrase meaning 'what came/what befell' (چه = 'what', آمد = past 'came/happened'). ز اختر به سر is a prepositional phrase 'from the star (اختر) onto/in our heads' — به سر is an idiom meaning 'upon the head' i.e. 'befell us from the star.'
segments.s768-g45: ازین is از + این ('from these'); the demonstrative is joined to the preposition in poetry. مارخوار is a compound epithet 'snake‑eater' (used as a pejorative epithet), and اهرمن چهرگان is a nominal phrase 'Ahriman‑faced' (اهرمن used adjectivally, چهرگان = 'faces/visages' plural), describing those people. The phrase literally means 'from these snake‑eating, Ahriman‑faced faces/people.'
segments.s768-g46: ز here functions like 'of/from' in the sense used in Persian phrases meaning 'devoid of' when combined with بی‑ compounds: 'ز دانایی و شرم بی‌بهرگان' = 'deprived of wisdom and shame.' دانایی و شرم are the two nouns ('wisdom' and 'shame/modesty'), and بی‌بهرگان is a plural nominal adjective meaning 'the deprived / those lacking (in these qualities).' Word order places the qualification after the nouns in a compact poetic form.
segments.s768-g47: نه ... نه ... is the correlative negation 'neither ... nor ...' repeated for emphasis before the listed nouns. The sequence lists goods of value: گنج (treasure), نام (name/fame), تخت (throne), نژاد (lineage). The structure enumerates what will not be preserved or given.
segments.s768-g48: همی‌داد is an imperfective/formal classical form 'kept giving' or 'was giving'; when combined with خواهند it yields a future/habitual sense ('they will give/they will keep on giving'). خواهِنـد is the future/auxiliary 'they will.' گیتی is the theme 'the world,' and بباد is a fused old verbal phrase meaning 'to be blown away / to be ruined' — together the clause means 'they will give the world to ruin' (i.e. the world will be lost/ruined).
segments.s768-g1: یکی (one) begins the clause 'one (of them) wrote a letter'. بنوشت is a poetic/past form of نوشت (to write) with the archaic prefix بـ; کرد/auxiliary is omitted because the simple past بنوشت stands on its own. دیگر after the verb marks 'the other (one)' and is followed by the name بطوس in apposition: 'one wrote a letter; the other, Batōs...'.
segments.s768-g2: پر از is the fixed phrase 'full of' (پر + از). خونِ دل is a genitive/compound 'blood of the heart' (i.e. metaphorical inner/bitter blood). و links روی ('and [his] face'); چون is a simile 'like/as', and سندروس is used metaphorically (a fragrant/sandalwood-like thing) to describe the face. The clause describes Batōs's condition: soaked with heart-blood and with a face like sandalwood.
segments.s768-g3: نخست is an adverb 'first/at first'. آفرین here is a noun 'praise/acclamation' used with کرد (to make/do) so آفرین کرد means 'praised'. بر marks the addressee/recipient ('to/upon') and دادگر is the object 'the just one/the just ruler.'
segments.s768-g4: کزو is a relative/adverbial pronoun 'from which/from whom' linking to the previous clause; دید is simple past 'he saw/beheld'. نیرو و بخت و هنر are a coordinated list of nouns ('strength/power, fortune, and skill/virtue') serving as the things he perceived in/with that just one.
segments.s768-g5: خداوند is used as an invocation/subject 'Lord/God'; the nouns پیروزی و فرهی ('victory and nobility/splendor') are its objects. The verb of blessing/wishing is omitted (typical in poetry), so the line reads as a prayer: 'May the Lord (give) victory and nobility.'
segments.s768-g6: The second خداوند repeats the vocative/subject. دیهیم is an archaic adjective 'of the land/rural' or 'folk-related' modifying the following phrase; شاهنشهی (kingship/imperial rule) completes the prayer: 'May the Lord of the land (grant) imperial kingship.' As before, the verbal element is omitted but understood.
segments.s768-g7: پی ... تا forms a range 'from ... to ...'. پی پشه here evokes the very small ('the gnat/insignificant creature') and تا introduces the upper extreme. پر و چنگ عقاب is 'the feather and the talon of the eagle' (پر and چنگ joined by و and governed by عقاب), contrasting small and great to show the full scope.
segments.s768-g8: به خشکی 'on/over the dry land' (به + noun), چو is the simile marker 'like/as', پیل 'elephant' gives the land-comparison; و links the second member 'نهنگ' (whale) and اندر آب 'in the water' (اندر = archaic 'in/within'). The phrase contrasts his prowess on land (like an elephant) and in the water (like a whale).
segments.s768-g89: کنون is an adverb placed at the start meaning 'now'. کشمگان پور is a patronymic: پور is the genitival element 'son (of)', so کشمگان پور = 'son of Kashmigan'; آن is a demonstrative/epithet 'that/the', and رزمخواه is an appositional noun 'war‑seeker/aspiring fighter' describing him. Word order is typical poetic Persian: time adverb → name+patronymic → demonstrative + epithet.
segments.s768-g90: بر ما بیامد is a verbal phrase: بیامد is simple past 'came' and بر ما = 'toward/to us' (بر here marks motion toward the speaker). بدین بارگاه is the destination: بدین = ب + دین in the text, functioning together as 'to this' (archaic/poetic 'to this'), and بارگاه = 'court/palace' is the object. The clause as a whole means 'came to our court.'
segments.s768-g91: بگفت is past-tense third‑person 'he said'. آنچ آمد is a relative-like cluster meaning 'that which came/occurred' (آنچ = 'that which', آمد = 'came/arose'). ز is the poetic preposition 'from', and شایستگی = 'worthiness/merit' names the source. Together: 'he said that which arose from worthiness.'
segments.s768-g92: هم ... هم is the correlative pair 'both ... and ...'. از / ز are synonymous poetic prepositions 'from'. بندگی here means 'devotion/servitude' (spiritual or loyal service) and بایستگی means 'necessity/obligation' — the speaker lists two sources or reasons. The construction reads 'both from devotion and from obligation.'
segments.s768-g93: شیندیم is first‑person plural perfect/past 'we placed/caused to sit' (here: 'we stationed' or 'we set'). زین مرزها functions adverbially/locatively as 'on these borders/frontiers' (زین = archaic/poetic 'these' or 'on these' in context). هرچ گفت strings in a relative/indefinite sense 'whatever (there) was/whatever was said/whatever was present' — poetic compression of 'هرچه بود/هرچه گفتند'. Word order is compact and typical of epic style.
segments.s768-g94: A series of nouns listing terrain/features: بلندی = 'heights' (here a noun meaning 'elevated places'), و = 'and', پستی = 'low places/lowlands' (here 'low terrain'), و = 'and', غار = 'caves', و = 'and', نهفت = 'hidden (places)'. The sequence names the kinds of locations where they were stationed.
segments.s768-g95: This is a locational range: دژ = 'fortress', گنبدین is an adjective 'domed' (modifying the following noun), کوه = 'mountain'. تا here means 'up to / as far as', and خرمنه = 'threshing‑floor' or 'stack/harvest place'. The phrase lists the span of places (from fortress/domed mountain as far as the threshing‑floor).
segments.s768-g96: دگر = 'another' or 'again'; لاژوردین = 'lapis‑lazuli/azure' used adjectivally or as material ('lapis‑blue'); ز بهر is an archaic purposive construction 'for the sake of / for'; بنه here means a small heap/mound (the second sense) — so together 'another lapis‑blue (mound) for the heap/for making a mound' or more naturally 'another lapis‑blue mound was placed.'
segments.s768-g49: بسی is an adverb/quantifier meaning 'much' or 'many' modifying the following noun phrase. گنج و گوهر is a coordinated noun phrase 'treasure and gems'. پراگنده is a past‑participle/adjectival verb 'scattered' and شد is the past/resultative auxiliary 'became/was', so the clause literally reads 'much treasure and gems became scattered' (i.e. 'were scattered').
segments.s768-g50: بسی again quantifies the following phrase. سر is the noun 'head(s)'. به خاک اندر is a prepositional expression 'into/on the dust' (به + خاک; اندر is a poetic/prepositional element reinforcing 'in/into'). آگنده is a participle/adjective 'filled/covered' and شد is the past auxiliary, so the phrase means 'many heads became covered/laid in the dust' (i.e. humbled/bowed).
segments.s768-g51: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' setting the manner. گشت is the past tense of گشتن 'turned / happened' (here 'turned'). پرگار is the noun 'compass' used metaphorically (the maker of circles), and چرخ بلند is a post‑nominal description 'of the lofty heaven' (classical Persian often omits ezāfe; read as 'پرگارِ چرخِ بلند' = 'the compass of the high heavens'). The clause reads 'thus turned the compass of the high heaven.'
segments.s768-g52: که introduces a result/explanatory clause 'that/so that'. آید is the present/subjunctive of آمدن used here to express resulting/hypothetical motion 'may/would come'. بدین is the prepositional phrase 'to/onto this' (constructed from prefix ب- + 'this'); پادشاهی is 'kingdom/kingship', and گزند is the noun 'harm'. The sequence means 'that harm would come to this kingdom.'
segments.s768-g53: ازین is the compound prepositional phrase 'from these' (از + این). زاغ‌ساران is a plural/noun 'company or flock of crows' used figuratively for a dark or worthless throng. بی‌آب و رنگ is a compound adjective 'without water and color' (figuratively 'lifeless, colorless, without substance'). Together they form 'from these crow‑flocks, lifeless and colorless...'.
segments.s768-g54: نه ... نه ... is a repeated negation coordinating 'neither ... nor ...'. هوش 'intelligence/awareness' and دانش 'knowledge' are the first pair negated; نام 'name/reputation' and ننگ 'shame/disgrace' are the second pair. The pattern enumerates the qualities these people lack: 'neither wit nor learning, neither fame nor shame.'
segments.s768-g55: که here is the relative/complementizer 'that/which' introducing the clause. نوشین‌روان is an epithet 'sweet‑souled' functioning as the subject. دیده بود is the compound past (دیده + بود) meaning 'had seen' (past perfective); این is the demonstrative 'this' (referring to the vision), and به خواب is the prepositional phrase 'in a dream' (به = 'in/into' here). So: 'which the sweet‑souled one had seen in a dream.'
segments.s768-g56: کزین is the classical compound 'from this' (ک + از + این). تخت is 'throne'. بپراگند is a subjunctive/jussive‑flavored verb form (ب‑ prefix + پراگند) expressing that something will/become scattered — here used as a potential/resultative 'would be scattered'. رنگ و آب are the coordinated nouns 'color and water' (آب here often figurative for life/essence). The phrase reads 'that from this throne color and water would be scattered.'
segments.s768-g57: چنان دید is a literary construction: چنان (an adverb, “thus/so”) modifies دید (past tense ‘saw’). کز is the archaic/poetic relative/conjunction (from که ز) introducing the clause 'that/from which', so the phrase literally begins the content of what he saw: 'that from the Arabs a hundred thousand...'. Word order follows classical Persian: adverb + verb, then the relativizer and the source/agent.
segments.s768-g58: هیونان is the head noun ‘beasts/animals’ (the things coming from the Arabs). مست و گسسته مهار are predicate adjectives/participial phrases: مست (figuratively ‘intoxicated/wild’) linked by و, and گسسته modifies مهار (literally ‘reins broken’). Note the participle (گسسته) precedes the noun it qualifies (مهار) — a flexible, poetic order meaning 'with reins broken'.
segments.s768-g59: گذر یافتندی is an older/poetic past-tense plural: گذر (passage) + یافتندی ('they found/obtained'), together functioning as 'they found passage' = 'they passed'. به marks motion into a place, so به اروند‌رود = 'into the Arvand River.' The verb is past, plural/archaic form.
segments.s768-g60: نماندی is the past verb meaning 'remained/was left' (used impersonally here: 'there remained'). برین is the fused prepositional phrase بر + ین (ین = orthographic variant of این ‘this’), so برین بوم = 'on this land'. بر تار و پود is an idiomatic locution meaning 'on warp and weft' (i.e. nothing of the fabric/estate remained); تار و پود are the warp and weft of woven cloth, used metaphorically.
segments.s768-g61: به ایران و بابل sets the locations ('in Iran and Babylon') with به marking place/target. نه ... و ... is the correlative negative 'neither ... nor,' so نه کشت و درود means 'neither cultivation (crops) nor greeting(s)'. The nouns follow the negative marker, forming a verbless clause typical of poetry about what did or did not exist.
segments.s768-g62: به چرخ زحل uses به + چرخ (heaven/sky) + زحل (Saturn) — 'to/onto Saturn's heavens.' برشدی is a poetic past verb meaning 'mounted/rose up' (literally 'went up'), so the clause says that تیره‌دود (black smoke) rose up to that sky. The noun تیره‌دود (a compound) is the thing doing the mounting.
segments.s768-g63: هم (also/even) emphasizes آتش (the sacred fire) as subject. بمردی here functions as a past verb meaning 'died/ perished' (an archaic/poetic form of مردن); because dictionary senses given do not match this verbal usage, the sense index is marked -1. به آتشکده is a locative phrase 'in/to the fire-temple' (به marking place).
segments.s768-g64: شدی is past of شدن ('became') used here in third person (poetic form). تیره ('dark/bleak') is the predicate adjective describing نوروز و جشن سده (Nowruz and the Sadeh festival). The coordination with و links the two festivals that 'became dark' — a succinct poetic result clause.
segments.s768-g97: ز is the poetic short form of از meaning 'from' or 'in (every)'. Here it introduces the manner: 'from/with every kind'. هر گونه is a genitive-style noun phrase 'every kind/manner'. بنمود is the past simple (3rd person singular) of نمودن 'to show/manifest' and takes آن دل گسل as its subject: آن دل‌گسل is an epithet 'that heart‑splitter / heart‑breaker' (آن = 'that/the', دل = 'heart', گسل used adjectivally). The overall chunk means 'in every way that that heart‑breaker displayed.'
segments.s768-g98: ز here functions as 'by/because of'. خوبی means 'beauty / good qualities' and is the cause/instrument. نمود is again the past verb 'showed / revealed' (here used in the sense 'revealed'). آنچ is a relative/pronominal compound 'that which / what', and بودش is بود + enclitic ش (بود + -ش) 'it was his/her' or 'it was (in) it'; together آنچ بودش به دل = 'what was in her heart'. به دل = 'in the heart' (به used spatially/figuratively).
segments.s768-g99: وزین in classical poetry is a fused form (و + زین) but can be read here as 'and from this / and with this' or as an epithet 'this dignified/weighty'. جایگه is a noun 'place/post/position'. شد is the past 'became' (3rd person singular). بهر جای کس is a prepositional phrase 'for the place of someone' (بهر = 'for', جای = 'post', کس = 'someone/another person'); together: 'and from this post it became someone else's.'
segments.s768-g100: پژوهنده here functions as an adjective/participle sense 'subject to inquiry / investigated' (literally 'inquirer' but used passively), followed by شد 'became' — i.e. 'became a matter of inquiry'. کارها is the plural 'the affairs/matters'. پیش و پس is a fixed pair meaning 'before and after' or 'back and forth, thoroughly' — so the clause says the affairs were examined in every direction.
segments.s768-g101: چنین is a demonstrative/adverb 'such' or 'so'. لشکری is 'an army / troops' (here indefinite noun). گشن is an adjective 'hungry'. ما را uses ما + را (emphatic/object marker) to indicate 'our' or 'of ours' in poetic usage. که هست is a relative-like connector + copula 'that is / which is' (here adding the descriptive clause: 'which is'). The whole chunk reads 'such an army of ours, which is hungry.'
segments.s768-g102: بر indicates position 'on/upon' (here 'confined to / sitting on'). ین is the poetic/orthographic form equivalent to این ('these'), so برین تنگ‌دژها = 'on these narrow forts'. تنگ‌دژها is the plural 'narrow forts / fortified passes'. نشاید is an impersonal modal 'it is not fitting / one should not'. نشست is the infinitive or verbal noun 'to sit / to settle' (i.e. 'to remain settled there'). Together: 'it is not fitting that (we) sit in/on these narrow forts.'
segments.s768-g103: نشستیم is the past 1st‑person plural of نشستن 'we sat / we met / we convened' (here 'we sat [in council]'). Note: the supplied lemma senses appear for a different root; grammatically it functions as 'we sat/assembled.' و = 'and'. گفتنیم is 1st‑person plural past 'we said / we spoke' (classical form گفتنیم = گفتیم). با = 'with'. رای‌زن is 'counselor / adviser' (one who gives رای 'opinion/counsel'). The clause: 'We sat and spoke with the counselors.'
segments.s768-g104: همه = 'all / everyone'. پهلوانان = plural 'champions / heroes'. شدند is the past plural 'they became / they were' (here 'they gathered / came to be'). انجمن is a noun 'assembly / council'. The short clause means 'all the champions became an assembly' i.e. 'all the champions gathered in council.'
segments.s768-g25: مبادا is a negative optative used to express a wish or prohibition ('may it not be' / 'lest'). گزند is the noun 'harm' and functions as the thing being wished away; سپهر بلند is a noun + adjective ('the lofty/heavenly sky'), with the adjective following the noun in classical Persian. The phrase reads literally as 'may there not be harm (to) the lofty sky.'
segments.s768-g26: This clause continues the wish-image: مه is used here in an intensive/epic sense 'mighty' (archaic/poetic), modifying پیکار 'battle'; آهرمن names the adversary (Ahriman), and پرگزند is an adjective meaning 'full of harm' modifying the preceding phrase. The verb 'be' is omitted (elliptical), so the clause reads as an optative/curse about Ahriman's battle being 'full of hurt.'
segments.s768-g27: همانا is a strong discourse particle ('verily' / 'behold'). شنیدند is a past-tense, 3rd-person-plural verb 'they heard'; classical Persian often places the verb before its subject, so گردنکشان ('the neck‑raisers', i.e. 'rebels/proud ones') is the subject that performed the hearing.
segments.s768-g28: خنیده here is a perfect/passive participial notion 'sung/chantered' and شد functions as the auxiliary/passive marker 'was' (here sense 'be (auxiliary)'). اندر جهان is a locative phrase 'in/throughout the world', and این نشان ('this sign') is the patient/theme; word order is verb + locative + theme, a common poetic inversion meaning 'this sign was chanted throughout the world.'
segments.s768-g29: که introduces a relative clause ('who/which/that'). بر کارزاری is a prepositional phrase 'on/at the battlefield' (بر + noun), و links the next phrase, and مرد نژاد means 'men of noble lineage' (مرد='man/hero', نژاد='lineage/race'); word order is noun phrases coordinated within the relative clause.
segments.s768-g30: دل ما is the topical noun phrase 'our heart' (noun + pronoun). پر‌آزرم is an adjectival/state predicate 'full of dignity/pride' describing دل, and the coordination و ... و links further predicates: مهرست is the fused form of 'مهر است' ('is love/affection' in classical contraction) and داد is the noun 'justice'. The clause thus predicates several qualities of 'our heart' (pride, love, justice); مهرست is an archaic contracted copula construction.
segments.s768-g31: به ویژه is an adverbial phrase 'especially'; نژاد شما را is a topicalized/marked object 'your lineage' (را marks it as definite/topic). که begins a relative clause modifying 'your lineage', and رنج is the head of that clause ('suffering' / 'hardship').
segments.s768-g32: فزونست is a stative verb 'is greater / increases' (classical present/past sense 'is more'). نزدیک here means 'when/being near' (or 'near to'), شاهان is 'kings', ز is the poetic preposition 'because of / from', and گنج is 'treasure'; together the clause says 'their suffering is greater when near kings on account of treasure.'
segments.s768-g65: This is a nominal chain introduced by the preposition 'از' (from). 'ایوان' (iwan/porch) is the place, modified by the title 'شاه جهان' (literally 'king of the world') which follows it as a compound; 'کنگره' (battlement) names the thing associated with the iwan. Poetic word order omits ezâfe links that prose would show (i.e. 'کنگرهٔ ایوانِ شاهِ جهان').
segments.s768-g66: 'فتادی' is an archaic/epic past tense form ('fell' — here third person singular, he/it fell). The prepositional phrase 'به میدان او' ('into/to his field/courtyard') follows the verb; note the possessive 'او' appears after the noun without an ezâfe (poetic omission). 'یکسره' is an adverb modifying the action (meaning 'straightaway/straight into').
segments.s768-g67: Adverb 'کنون' (now) opens the clause. 'خواب را' marks 'the dream' as the definite object with the object marker 'را'. The phrase 'پاسخ آمد پدید' is a poetic inversion (literal sequence 'answer came manifest'): 'آمد' (came/happened) plus 'پدید' (appeared/manifest) together mean 'an answer appeared'.
segments.s768-g68: The preposition 'ز' ('from/of') with 'ما' ('us') indicates the affected party or source ('from us' / 'for us'). 'بخت' (fate/destiny) is the grammatical subject, and 'گردن' (neck) is the object that fate is said to 'کشید' (take/pull). 'بخواهد' here expresses future/modal intent ('will/would desire'), so the clause means 'fate will seek to take our necks' — an idiomatic way of saying destiny will bring death or beheading. Poetic phrasing omits possessive marking on 'گردن' because 'ز ما' already establishes the relation.
segments.s768-g69: 'شود' (becomes/will be) is the verb governing the clause. The relative phrase 'هر کس که هست ارجمند' ('whoever is noble/esteemed') follows and functions as the subject; word order is flexible and poetic here. The result 'خوار' (despised/lowly) is predicated of that subject: 'whoever is esteemed will become despised.'
segments.s768-g70: This short clause puts the affected party 'فرومایه' (the low-born/base) before the object marker 'را' to emphasize them as the one changed. The verb phrase 'بخت گردد بلند' ('fortune becomes/high' = 'fortune is raised') follows; 'گردد' is a passive/neutral 'becomes' and 'بلند' is the result adjective. The construction means 'the low-born's fortune will be exalted.'
segments.s768-g71: 'پراگنده گردد' ('becomes widespread') is the verbal phrase; the adjective 'پراگنده' precedes the verb in poetic order. 'بدی در جهان' ('evil in the world') supplies the theme/location: 'evil becomes widespread in the world.' 'گردد' is present/future-like 'becomes' used for general statement.
segments.s768-g72: This phrase contrasts two states elliptically: 'گزند آشکارا' ('harm openly/evident') and 'خوبی نهان' ('goodness hidden'). The verbs are omitted by ellipsis (poetic compression), so the nouns pair with adverbs/adjectives to mean 'harm will be visible and goodness will be concealed.'
segments.s768-g113: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from'; the phrase lists the things not to be removed: 'زرینه' (golden/gilding) modifies 'جامه' (garment/robe). 'نابرید' is a negative imperative ('do not cut/tear'), placed at the end as the main verb governing the preceding objects.
segments.s768-g114: 'ز' again 'from'; 'چیزی' = 'something/anything' is the head of the relative clause introduced by 'که' ('that'). 'آن را' is the object with the definite/object marker 'را'. 'نشاید' is an impersonal/analytic expression 'it is not fitting' (classical present), and 'کشید' is the verb 'to pull/draw/tear' (here used after 'نشاید' to mean 'ought not to be pulled'). The clause therefore means 'from anything that ought not to be pulled.'
segments.s768-g115: 'هم' = 'also/too' adds this line; 'از خوردنیها' = 'from the edibles/foods' (the source). 'ز هر گونه' = 'of every kind' (the preposition 'ز' here is poetic 'of/from'), and 'ساز' is an imperative 'prepare/provide' (classical/concise imperative form placed after its objects), so the whole segment commands: 'also provide foods of every kind.'
segments.s768-g116: 'که' introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'which...'; 'ما را' is the experiencer/indirect object ('to/for us') marked by 'را'; 'بیاید' is the impersonal/classical expression 'be fitting' (i.e. 'suits'); 'برو' is an imperative 'go/act' and is followed by the prepositional phrase 'بر نیاز' ('according to/for the need'). Together: 'which suits us; go according to need.'
segments.s768-g117: 'ز' = 'from/of'; 'گاوان' is the plural 'oxen/cattle'; 'گردون‌کشان' is a poetic compound adjective literally 'sky‑dragging' or 'heaven‑pulling' (an epithet for very strong oxen); 'چل‌هزار' is the numeral 'forty‑thousand' modifying the whole noun phrase. Classical Persian often stacks adjectives/epithets before/after the noun in this way.
segments.s768-g118: 'که' introduces a clause describing the oxen: 'رنج آورد' = 'bring/cause toil' where 'رنج' (hardship) is the object and 'آورد' is the verb 'bring/cause'; 'تا' here = 'until' (introducing a temporal/result clause), the following 'که' is the clausal marker and 'آید' = 'come/happen'. 'به کار' = 'into work/service/use'. So: 'which cause hardship until they come into use.'
segments.s768-g119: 'به خروار' = 'by/into the kharvar' (a unit/measure), 'زان پس' = 'after that/thereupon', and the numeric phrase 'ده و دو هزار' is an archaic ordering of numerals meaning 'ten thousand and two thousand' (i.e. twelve thousand). Word order is compact and somewhat archaic; the numeral phrase follows the temporal phrase.
segments.s768-g120: 'به خوشه درون گندم' is a prepositional phrase 'into/unto the ear within the wheat' (poetic, specifying the grain form). 'آرد' here is a jussive/optative form 'may it bring/let it bring' (from 'آورد'), and 'ببار' is a hortative/imperative 'pour (down)/let it rain' — together a wish/command that wheat be produced in the ears and pour down abundantly.
segments.s768-g33: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the clause. بهرام چوبینه is a proper name (بهرام with the epithet چوبینه, literally 'of the club'). آمد پدید is a literary two-part expression: آمد 'came' + پدید 'forth/appeared', together 'came forth / appeared'. Word order is typical for classical Persian narrative: temporal word → subject → verbal phrase.
segments.s768-g34: ز indicates source/relation here ('from/against' or 'because of') introducing the thing opposed to. فرمان دیهیم ما is a noun phrase 'the command/authority of our (rural/land) [people]' with ما as possessor. سرکشید is the simple past of سرکشیدن meaning 'rose up / rebelled' and here agrees with the singular proper name (he rebelled).
segments.s768-g35: شما را دل uses را after شما to mark possession/affected possessor in classical style: 'your heart(s)'. از introduces the source 'from' and شهرهای فراخ is the noun phrase 'spacious/wide cities'. The whole phrase means 'your heart turned away from the wide cities.'
segments.s768-g36: بپیچید is a poetic/past narrative form of پیچیدن 'to turn' (here 'turned away'). In classical verse the full past plural ending is often dropped, so this form functions as past narrative. وز here is the fused sequence 'و ز' (= 'and from'), introducing the following list; it does not match the literal senses given in the glossary (hence marked separately below). باغ و میدان و کاخ are three coordinated items 'garden, square, and palace.'
segments.s768-g37: برین is the preposition بر 'on/upon' + the demonstrative-like element ین (here 'these/that'), giving 'upon these'. باستان modifies the following nouns: 'ancient'. راع here is a noun coordinated with کوه بلند (literally 'shepherd' or related pastoral term in the line) and together with 'کوه بلند' it lists the places 'the ancient pastures (or shepherding-places) and the lofty mountain.'
segments.s768-g38: کده ساختید is a past tense clause: کده 'dwellings/abodes' + ساختید (past 2nd-person plural) 'you built'. از marks source/purpose 'from / against', and نهیب گزند is a poetic phrase 'the roar/blast of harm' (نهیب here 'thunderous cry/blast', گزند 'harm'), meaning 'built dwellings to shelter from the blast/threat of harm.'
segments.s768-g39: گر‌ای combines the conditional particle گر 'if' with the vocative ای (here directed at دون 'O base/lowly one'); this is an address plus conditional. که introduces the content clause 'that God gives power': نیرو 'power/strength' is the object of دهد 'gives/grants', and کردگار is the agent 'the Creator/God'. دهد is present/subjunctive-marked form often used to express potential or wish in classical style.
segments.s768-g40: به کام دل ما شود is an idiomatic sequence: به کام دل شدن 'to come to one's heart's desire'. به marks the beneficiary/goal 'to/for', کام دل 'the heart's desire', ما 'our', and شود is a present/subjunctive 'may become' expressing a wished-for outcome. روزگار here means 'fortune/fate' (i.e. 'may fate become as our hearts desire').
